did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used transformer-based deep learning models (ViT, Swin, DeiT, and DINO) for binary classification of benign and malignant skin cancer using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2017 dataset. The study evaluated the effect of color space transformations (RGB, HSV, LAB, YCbCr) on the classification performance of these AI models.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the conversion of the ISIC 2017 dataset into different color spaces (RGB, HSV, LAB, YCbCr) and the training and evaluation of four specific transformer-based models (ViT, Swin, DeiT, DINO) for skin cancer classification. The paper includes a detailed methodology, dataset description, hyperparameter settings, quantitative results with metrics (accuracy, AUC, etc.), and Grad-CAM explainability analysis, which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: Comparative analysis of the effect of color space transformations on transformer-based skin cancer classification
brief_summary: This study investigated the impact of color space transformations on the performance of transformer-based models for binary skin cancer classification using the ISIC 2017 dataset. Dermoscopic images were converted from RGB to HSV, LAB, and YCbCr color spaces and evaluated using ViT, Swin, DeiT, and DINO architectures. The models were assessed via 5-fold cross-validation using metrics including accuracy, AUC, and balanced accuracy, alongside Grad-CAM explainability analysis. The DeiT model trained on RGB images achieved the highest classification performance with an accuracy of 0.7668 and an AUC of 0.8272. In contrast, ViT and DINO models showed lower and more fluctuating performance across the different color spaces. The results indicate that color space selection significantly influences transformer model efficacy, with the RGB-DeiT combination offering the most robust decision support for early malignancy diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as benign in the binary classification task using the ISIC 2017 dataset (2229 images).

Classified as malignant in the binary classification task using the ISIC 2017 dataset (521 images). The paper specifically highlights melanoma as the riskiest subtype of skin cancer in the introduction, but the experimental task is binary (benign vs. malignant).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (benign vs. malignant skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of skin cancer using the ISIC 2017 dataset. The models (ViT, Swin, DeiT, DINO) predict discrete labels for benign or malignant lesions. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, AUC, precision, recall, specificity, F1 score, and balanced accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A public dataset consisting of high-resolution dermoscopic images of skin lesions, used for binary classification of benign and malignant skin cancer.

use.training: Used for training transformer-based models (ViT, Swin, DeiT, DINO) with 60% of the data allocated to the training subset.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping with 20% of the data allocated to the validation subset.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation with 20% of the data allocated to the testing subset, evaluated via 5-fold cross-validation.
use.other: Images were converted from RGB to HSV, LAB, and YCbCr color spaces for comparative analysis.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure the generalization ability of transformer-based models and the reliability of classification results; reported performance values represent the mean of five independent test evaluations.
To report final performance metrics (Accuracy, AUC, etc.) as the average of the five independent test evaluations from the cross-validation folds.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using mean accuracy, AUC, and other metrics derived from 5-fold cross-validation without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2017.description: Binary classification of benign vs. malignant skin lesions using 5-fold cross-validation. Results represent the mean of five independent test evaluations.
ISIC 2017.notes: The DeiT model trained on RGB images achieved the highest performance with an accuracy of 0.7668 and an AUC of 0.8272. The Swin model achieved the highest accuracy in the HSV color space (0.7810) and the highest AUC in the RGB color space (0.8089). The ViT model achieved the highest balanced accuracy (0.7495) in the LAB color space. The DINO model showed the lowest performance across all color spaces, with accuracy below 0.55. ViT and DINO models were more sensitive to color space transformations compared to Swin and DeiT.

architecture_used.main_model: Four transformer-based models: Visual Transformer (ViT), Swin Transformer, Data Efficient Image Transformer (DeiT), and Label-free Self-Distillation (DINO).
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for the specific models used in the study, though DeiT is noted as leveraging distillation techniques and DINO uses self-supervised learning on unlabeled data in its general description.

architecture_details.ViT: Divides input image into 16x16 patches treated as tokens; uses positional embeddings and self-attention mechanisms to capture long-range dependencies and global context.
architecture_details.Swin: Divides images into non-overlapping local windows; uses shifted window mechanism combined with an attention module to capture local and global dependencies; maintains linear computational complexity.
architecture_details.DeiT: Leverages distillation techniques to improve training efficiency of ViT models, particularly effective in limited datasets.
architecture_details.DINO: Uses a self-supervised learning approach on the ViT model to learn visual representations from unlabeled data.

training_details.data_augmentation: Random horizontal/vertical flips and ±15° random rotations applied during training; only resizing and normalization applied to validation/test sets.

classification_or_output_head.details: Not explicitly detailed beyond the binary classification task description.

preprocessing_techniques: Color space transformations: Conversion of RGB images to HSV, LAB, and YCbCr color spaces
Resizing: Input images resized to 224 × 224 pixels
Normalization: Applied to validation and test sets
Data augmentation (training only): Random horizontal/vertical flips and ±15° random rotations

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during the training process to reduce overfitting. Not applied to validation and test datasets.
Applied during the training process with a range of ±15° to reduce overfitting. Not applied to validation and test datasets.

conclusions: The DeiT model trained on RGB images achieved the highest classification performance with an accuracy of 0.7668 and an AUC of 0.8272, making it the most robust model for skin cancer classification among those tested.

Color space selection significantly influences the efficacy of transformer-based models, with RGB and HSV color spaces generally yielding stable and high accuracy values, particularly for DeiT and Swin models.
The ViT and DINO models were more sensitive to color space transformations and achieved lower classification performance compared to Swin and DeiT.

The ViT model exhibited high sensitivity to color space transformations, with stable training processes in RGB and HSV but high fluctuations and instabilities in LAB and YCbCr spaces, indicating its attention mechanism struggles with separated brightness-color components.
The Swin model demonstrated stable and balanced training across all color spaces, achieving the highest accuracy (0.7810) in the HSV color space and the highest AUC (0.8089) in the RGB color space.

The DeiT model showed the most stable feature extraction against color space transformations, maintaining high accuracy and AUC values above 0.79 in RGB and YCbCr spaces with minimal F1 score fluctuations.

The DINO model showed the lowest performance across all color spaces, with accuracy below 0.55, low precision, and high validation loss, indicating a tendency to overfit and insufficiency for malignant classification in this context.

Grad-CAM explainability analysis confirmed that color space transformations affect where models focus in image regions during classification decisions, highlighting the usability of these transformations for interpretability in dermatological diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single dataset (ISIC 2017), which restricts the generalizability of the findings to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance comparisons between models or color spaces.

The DINO model demonstrated poor performance (accuracy < 0.55) across all color spaces, indicating it may be insufficient or prone to overfitting for this specific binary classification task without further tuning.
The dataset is imbalanced (2229 benign vs. 521 malignant), although balanced accuracy was used as a metric to mitigate this issue.

Data leakage risk is mitigated by the use of 5-fold cross-validation where test data in each fold was excluded from training and validation, with final metrics reported as the mean of five independent evaluations.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets, relying solely on internal cross-validation within the ISIC 2017 dataset.

The DeiT model achieved the highest performance (Accuracy: 0.7668, AUC: 0.8272) in RGB space, but these absolute performance levels may be considered modest for clinical decision support systems requiring high sensitivity.
No hyperparameter tuning was performed across different color spaces; all models used identical hyperparameters, which may not represent the optimal configuration for each specific color space.
